coredata.dc:title: How porosity affects the emission of fluorescent carbon dot-silica porous composites
coredata.dc:creator: Carbonaro, Carlo Maria
Thakkar, Swapneel Vijay
Ludmerczki, Róbert
Olla, Chiara
Pinna, Andrea
Loche, Danilo
Malfatti, Luca
Cesare Marincola, Flaminia
Casula, Maria Francesca
coredata.dc:description: Blue-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDN) produced by a solvent-free method have been successfully incorporated into silica (SiO2) through a sol-gel procedure. By exploiting the co-gelation of the dispersed carbon dots and of the silica matrix, homogeneous distribution of the carbon dots was obtained throughout the matrix. The CDN-SiO2 composites in the form of xero-, cryo-, and aerogels featuring different porous textures were produced by exploiting different gel drying strategies. The most critical parameters and the material features associated to the different synthesis were investigated by transmission electron microscopy, thermal analysis, Mid-infrared spectroscopy, N2 physisorption isotherms and time-resolved spectrofluorimetry. The emissive properties of CDN-SiO2 composites in the form of aero-, cryo- and xerogel were studied. The spectral properties and the efficiency of the emission change in the CDN-SiO2 composites pointed at the effect of CD loading and porous texture of xerogels. Our investigation expands the exploitation of functional carbon dots through the production of fluorescent solid-state composites with tunable porosities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Aerogel
Composite
Cryogel
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dot-silica porous composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support of Università di Cagliari and Fondazione di Sardegna, project ADVANCING, CUP F74I19001030007 and project NG-Light, CUP F74I19000930007 and Italian Ministry of Education, University and Research (MIUR) project PRIN2017 “CANDL2” (Grant 2017W75RAE), are gratefully acknowledged. SVT thanks financial support under P.O.R. Sardegna F.S.E.-Operational Programme of the Autonomous Region of Sardinia, European Social Fund 2014-2020- Axis III Education and training, Thematic goal 10, Investment Priority 10ii- Specific goal 10.5.12. We acknowledge the CeSAR (Centro Servizi Ricerca d'Ateneo) core facility of the University of Cagliari and Dr. A. Ardu for assistance with the generation of TEM images on a Jeol JEM 1400 Plus microscope.

The financial support of Università di Cagliari and Fondazione di Sardegna , project ADVANCING, CUP F74I19001030007 and project NG-Light, CUP F74I19000930007 and Italian Ministry of Education, University and Research (MIUR) project P RIN2017 “CANDL2” (Grant 2017W75RAE ), are gratefully acknowledged. SVT thanks financial support under P.O.R. We acknowledge the CeSAR (Centro Servizi Ricerca d'Ateneo) core facility of the University of Cagliari and Dr. A. Ardu for assistance with the generation of TEM images on a Jeol JEM 1400 Plus microscope.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Visual appearance of plain SiO2 alcogel (a) and of CDN-SiO2 composite alcogel under day light (b) and UV light (c).

FT-IR spectrum of the obtained CDN (a), false colored 3D photoluminescence spectrum of CDN in water (1 mg/L, excitation/y-axis, emission/x-axis) (b); UV–Vis spectrum of CDN in water (50 mg/L) (c); and TEM image (d) of the CDN (scale bar is 100 nm).

N2 physisorption isotherms at 77 K (a, b, c) and TEM images (d, e, f) for the CDN-SiO2 composites in the form of xerogel, cryogel, and aerogel, respectively. Scale bar for main TEM images is 100 nm, for insets is 50 nm.

Mid-Infrared Fourier Transform spectra of the xerogel (a), cryogel (b), and aerogel (c) plain SiO2 and CDN-SiO2 composites . (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Emission spectra (from left to right) of the xerogel, cryogel, and aerogel CDN-SiO2 composites.
Excitation spectra of the CDN sample at the emission peak of the emission spectra fit results.
Decay time plot of CDN-SiO2 composites in the form of xerogel, cryogel, and aerogel.
Textural parameters for the CDN-SiO2 composites as obtained by N2 physisorption.

Pre-exponential factors (Ai), lifetimes (τi), fractional contributions (f i) and average lifetimes (⟨τ⟩), as estimated by time resolved PL measurements for the xero-, cryo- and aerogel CDN-SiO2 composites.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Ai is the pre-exponential factor, τi is the lifetime, f i is the fractional contribution, and ⟨τ⟩ is the average lifetime; see the Supporting Information for details.

head.ce:title: How porosity affects the emission of fluorescent carbon dot-silica porous composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: KAUST, King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST), Biological and Environmental Sciences and Engineering (BESE) Division, NABLA Lab, 23955-6900, Thuwal, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDN) produced by a solvent-free method have been successfully incorporated into silica (SiO2) through a sol-gel procedure. By exploiting the co-gelation of the dispersed carbon dots and of the silica matrix, homogeneous distribution of the carbon dots was obtained throughout the matrix. The CDN-SiO2 composites in the form of xero-, cryo-, and aerogels featuring different porous textures were produced by exploiting different gel drying strategies. The most critical parameters and the material features associated to the different synthesis were investigated by transmission electron microscopy, thermal analysis, Mid-infrared spectroscopy, N2 physisorption isotherms and time-resolved spectrofluorimetry. The emissive properties of CDN-SiO2 composites in the form of aero-, cryo- and xerogel were studied. The spectral properties and the efficiency of the emission change in the CDN-SiO2 composites pointed at the effect of CD loading and porous texture of xerogels. Our investigation expands the exploitation of functional carbon dots through the production of fluorescent solid-state composites with tunable porosities.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of functional nanoparticles whose relevance is mainly related to the advantageous optical properties such as stable and tunable photoluminescence combined to affordable and cost-effective production [1]. CDs can be regarded as carbon-based nanometer-confined particles, which are dominated by sp2 carbon and contain a large number of surface groups such as carboxyls and hydroxyls, which account for the great hydrophilic behavior of CDs. The origin of the photoluminescence in CDs is not fully understood and is currently under investigation. The close resemblance in the optical behavior of CDs with that of molecular fluorophores such as citrazinic acid and its derivatives and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons has been proposed as a way to gain insights into the emission properties of CDs [2]. Accounted models for the origin of the emission in CDs acknowledge the contribution from core and surface effects [3,4]. In particular, concerning chemical and structural effects, it is noteworthy that doping with nitrogen atoms is regarded as an efficient way to enhance the quantum efficiency of CDs, up to 80% and even above [5,6], and to promote emission features across the visible range depending on the nitrogen-carbon coordination [7].

The emission properties of CDs have already been successfully applied to many areas, with major focus on environment and biomedicine in the field of bio-imaging [5,8], ion-detection [9,10], photocatalysis [11,12], and photo-thermal therapy [5]. At present, however, performing results have been limited to solution-based applications, as several drawbacks such as quenching of the fluorescence, poor processability, nanoparticle aggregation, and loss of stability are encountered in dry CD powders or concentrated media. Such limitations are mostly critical in view of the potential of CDs in optoelectronics and in particular in the development of novel solid-state light emitting device (LED) technologies [13]. In this framework, silica has been proposed as a matrix for the preparation of CD-SiO2 composites, in the light of its chemical and thermal stability, relevance for optical and lighting devices, as well as well-established production technology [14,15]. In particular, sol-gel routes have recently been suggested based on the unique advantage of ensuring high purity, homogeneity in the distribution of the dispersed phase over the matrix, and control over fine properties such as texture and porosity. The use of specific gel drying conditions is a valuable strategy to tune the texture of sol-gel materials. As an alternative to conventional evaporative drying, which leads to the formation of microporous silica, procedures relying on freeze drying or supercritical drying of the wet gel can be performed to produce low density silicates in the form of cryogel and aerogel, respectively [16,17].

To address the preparation of CD-silica composite gels, one successful approach has been proposed by taking advantage of the well-developed strategies to embed optically active materials within silica spheres by Stöber methods [18–21]. Silica nanospheres loaded with CDs were assembled in the form of xerogels; by this procedure, being the CD isolated by the thick silica shell, no quenching of the optical properties was recorded even at high CD loading [22]. As an alternative approach, organosilica and organosilane-modified CDs were used as precursors to produce composites in the form of xerogels and films, which can be regarded as candidates for scintillation detectors based on the observed energy transfer from the matrix to CD [23,24]. Finally, to take advantage of the unique textural features of aerogels, the preparation of CD-SiO2 composite aerogels has been attempted by impregnation of a pre-formed powdered silica aerogel by a solution of pre-formed carbon dots, and the resulting porous composites have been exploited as gas sensors [25]. Impregnation procedures, however, tend to suffer from poor flexibility in terms of compositional control, due to limitations in the degree of homogeneity of the dispersed phase distribution into the matrix, and in the tunability of dispersed phase loading in the composite. Improved sol-gel routes for the preparation of CD-SiO2 composite aerogels include the use of pre-formed SiO2 aerogel which is then immersed into a solution of CD precursors and submitted to hydrothermal treatment [26], and co-gelation of silica and pre-formed carbon dots [27]. The latter method enabled to prepare composites with different CD loading, which was used to assess the optimal composition in view of aniline gas detection as compared to competitive quenching effects. The produced composite, however, exhibits a relatively dense structure (pore volume lower than 0.7 cm3 g−1, pore size 3.4 nm) as compared to silica aerogels.

In this work, we investigate the production of luminescent composites with tunable porosity and textural features. We make use of the co-gelation of blue-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDN) and of an alkoxysilane to obtain a composite wet gel (alcogel), which is then submitted to controlled drying conditions to produce CDN-SiO2 porous composites in the form of xero-, cryo- and aerogels. Textural and morphological investigation is used to assess the specific features of the composites, to compare them with those of corresponding SiO2 xero-, cryo- and aerogels, and to interpret the spectroscopic features of the luminescent composites, which are assessed by time-resolved photoluminescence. It is demonstrated that dispersion into highly porous matrices is an effective strategy to develop carbon dot-based solid emitting materials.

TEOS (C8H20O4Si, Tetraethyl orthosilicate, reagent grade 98%), HCl (37%), and urea (CH4N2O, ACS reagent >98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, whereas absolute ethanol (CH3CH2OH, ACS reagent 99.8%), and citric acid monohydrate (>99.5%) were from Fluka.

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDN) were prepared starting from citric acid and urea, by optimization of a solvent-free procedure recently developed in our laboratory [6,28]. Briefly, 5.25 g (0.025 mol) of citric acid and 3.00 g (0.050 mol) urea were placed in a 100 mL round bottomed flask, immersed into a preheated 180 °C oil bath and stirred at 100 rpm until melting, which occurred within 5 min. The melt was then let to react for 45 min, and the so-obtained black reaction mixture was allowed to cool and solidify at ambient temperature. The crude product CDN was used without further purification for processing and characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dot-silica porous composites
ce:section.ce:para: CDN-SiO2 composites in the form of xerogel, aerogel and cryogel were prepared starting from the same wet gel (alcogel) by adopting the appropriate gel drying procedure. Typical alcogel preparation was carried out by pre-hydrolyzing an ethanolic solution of TEOS which is used as silica precursor (2.63 mL of TEOS in 1 mL EtOH) with a hydroalcoholic acidic solution (HNO3/EtOH/H2O in ratios 1:10:40 by volume) under stirring at 50 °C for 30 min. The solution was cooled down under stirring and 7 mg of CDN dispersed in 2.5 mL of EtOH by sonication for 10 min were added to the mixture. Finally, a hydroalcoholic solution of urea (Urea/EtOH/H2O in ratios 4:1:18 in mmol ratio) was added, and the mixture left under reflux at 85 °C to promote basic hydrolysis. After 60 min, the viscous sol was transferred to a cylindric mold, capped and incubated at 40 °C in an oven under static air to produce the wet gel. This procedure was previously shown to be effective in the production of all-inorganic silica-based composites and lead to homogeneous and crack-free alcogels [29,30, ]. CDN-SiO2 composites were obtained in the form of xerogels by further heating at 180 °C for 3 h under static air and will be hereafter labeled X-CDN-SiO2. Aerogels (A-CDN-SiO2 composites) were produced by supercritical drying of the alcogel. This was achieved by introducing the alcogel in a Parr autoclave filled with 70 mL of ethanol and extensively flushed with N2 gas. The autoclave was placed in the corresponding heating mantle and heated until the temperature was around 330 °C and the pressure around 70 atm, at which point the autoclave was slowly vented until ambient pressure and further flushed with N2. CDN-SiO2 composites in the form of cryogels (C-CDN-SiO2) were obtained by submitting the alcogel to extensive solvent exchange procedure in order to replace ethanol with water. In detail, a gel of ~12 mL volume was placed in 250 mL DI water overnight to facilitate the exchange, and the step was performed twice to ensure complete replacement of ethanol with water. During this step, some leakage of CDN was observed as ethanol was removed from the wet gel. The gel was frozen at −20 °C and then dried overnight in a Virtis BenchTop 6.6 ES Freeze Dryer at −56 °C and 50 mTorr. The same procedure described above was used to prepare plain silica samples in the form of xero-, aero-, and cryogels (X-SiO2, A-SiO2 and C-SiO2) which were used as a reference.

N2 physisorption experiments at 77 K were performed using a Micrometrics ASAP 2020 porosimeter, and the surface area and pore parameters were assessed by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda methods, respectively [31].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a Hitachi H-7000 and on a Jeol JEM 1400 Plus microscope. Finely ground samples were deposited for observation on carbon-coated or on removable formvar silicon monoxide copper grids, both purchased from Ted Pella, Inc.
Fourier Transform Mid-Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded in the 400–4000 cm−1 range in transmission mode on samples dispersed in KBr pellets using a Bruker Equinox 55 spectrometer.
Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements were collected on a Netzsch STA 449 F1, from room temperature to 800 °C at 10 °C∙min−1 under air flow.

Time resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) measurements were recorded by exciting the samples with 200 fs long pulses delivered by an optical parametric amplifier (Light Conversion TOPAS-C) pumped by a regenerative Ti:sapphire amplifier (Coherent Libra-HE). The repetition frequency was 1 kHz and the PL signal was recovered by a streak camera (Hamamatsu C10910) equipped with a grating spectrometer (Princeton Instruments Acton SpectraPro SP-2300). All the measurements were collected in the front face configuration to reduce inner filter effects. Proper optical filters were applied to remove the reflected contribution of the excitation light.

The one-pot synthetic procedure adopted for the preparation of the CDN-SiO2 composite was effective in producing highly homogeneous alcogels. Fig. 1 shows a plain SiO2 alcogel, and a CDN-SiO2 composite alcogel as imaged under day light and under UV light: the homogeneous brown color and the photoluminescence suggest that CDN exhibit an even distribution within the silica matrix and retain their optical functionalities. This result is due to the ease of integration of CDN into sol-gel synthetic protocols thanks to their dispersibility into polar solvents such as ethanol.

Indeed, carbon dots were found to be highly hydrophilic due to the abundance of -COOH and -NH2 surface functional groups as detected by IR spectroscopy (see Fig. 2 a). FTIR spectroscopy was also used to confirm the effective reaction among the used precursors: the CDN sample (Fig. 2a) indicates the absence of the characteristic signals of citric acid monohydrate and urea in the highlighted IR regions. The dominant signals of precursors which can be used to check the reaction are mainly due to the O-H stretching of the carboxyl groups of citric acid [32], the N-H stretching of urea [33], as well as the three C=O signals of citric acid monohydrate [34] and the C=O and N-H signals of urea [35], as detailed in Figure SI-1. Studies of the thermal decomposition of urea alone and of citric acid alone have identified the most relevant involved species [34,36]. In the investigated system, since urea was used in excess, it is more likely to find some products of urea pyrolysis than the ones from citric acid pyrolysis. The synthesis of carbon dots from citric acid and urea was also studied by Kasprzyk et al. [37] and confirmed citrazinic acid (and its amide) as a source of fluorescence. Likewise, the investigated CDN sample shows resemblance to citrazinic acid (Fig. 2b). In particular, citrazinic acid shows strong fluorescence peaking at around 445 nm, and its UV-spectrum resembles to that recorded for CDN (Fig. 2c), featuring two characteristic bands at around 230 and 340 nm due to π → π∗ of conjugated π electrons in aromatic fluorophores, and n → π∗ transitions of the functional groups on the C-dots surface. Despite the poor contrast, CDN can be imaged under TEM (Fig. 2d) which indicates nearly spherical particles with size below 10 nm.

The alcogel having a volume of ~12 cm3 had a mass of ~10 g: supercritical drying occurred with negligible volume shrinkage, while a mass loss of ~90% was recorded. The apparent density of the aerogel was therefore around 0.07 g cm−3. A similar weight loss was recorded for the cryogel; some volume shrinkage was observed at the end of the solvent exchange process to approximately 10 cm3. During this step, some CDN leakage from the alcogel was also observed as a result of the solvent exchange process. Upon lyophilization, a very light and soft cryogel was obtained which retained roughly the same volume as the wet gel but tended to separate into powder very easily. Although the volume of the cryogel could not be determined, it can be roughly estimated that the apparent density was around 0.09 g cm−3. These observations (see also Fig. SI-2) suggested very different textural properties of the materials in the form of xero-, cryo-, and aerogels, as expected. The texture and morphology of the materials was further investigated by N2 physisorption. The physisorption isotherms shown in Fig. 3 (a–c) suggest different porous features for the three composites [31,38]. In particular, the xerogel exhibits a composite isotherm due to a type I (microporous) and type IVa contribution, which can be ascribed to the occurrence of micropores and small mesopores. The cryogel shows a type IIb isotherm with a contribution of micropores discernible at low relative pressures; whereas the aerogel isotherm is of type IVa accompanied by a type H1 hysteresis loop lying at high relative pressure values, due to large mesopores with relatively narrow distribution. Table 1 reports the main textural parameters of the CDN-SiO2 composites as derived from physisorption data. The highly porous texture of the materials results in high surface areas, which vary depending on the form of the material. In particular, the highest values in terms of pore volume is recorded for the aerogel, reflecting its open and large mesopores. TEM investigation was performed to gain further insights on the pore texture of the materials. Representative images reported in Fig. 3(d–f) support that the prepared materials are porous, although different textures are obtained. In particular, X-CDN-SiO2 appears as relatively dense, and small micropores can be detected. The cryogel composite has somehow a similar appearance, with small pores more easily discernible. Finally, A-CDN-SiO2 composites exhibit a very open texture featuring large mesopores. Due to the size and poor contrast associated to the carbon dots, the CDN are very hard to detect within the composites. This is particularly the case of the relatively denser composites, whereas in the aerogel composite some contrast is observed which however cannot be unambigously associated to the occurrence of the carbon dots and hence no definitive information on CDN location within the porous matrix can be inferred by TEM.

The corresponding xero-, cryo-, and aerogel plain SiO2 materials have also been investigated as a reference. The N2 physisorption and TEM data are reported in Fig. SI-3. The A-SiO2 does not show any significant difference as compared to the corresponding composite aerogel, exhibiting the typical fine texture of the silica backbone giving rise to open mesoporosity. On the other hand, X-SiO2 shows a typical microporous texture, as pointed out by a type Ib physisorption loop and by the relatively fine and dense texture pointed out by TEM. The C-SiO2 shows a coarser texture as compared to A-SiO2 and the occurrence of both small and larger mesopores.

These data point out again that the drying procedure affects the porosity of the final material, ranging from a microporous xerogel to a coarser cryogel, to a highly porous aerogel, which also reflects in the values of pore volume (see Table SI-1). Likely, due to the texture associated to large mesopores, in the case of aerogels the addition of CDN does not vary the porous texture. On the other hand, in the xero- and cryo-gels, due to their smaller pores, differences in the textural features are found in the composites as compared to the plain SiO2. These data indicate that although the texture of the composite is mainly affected by the drying technique including solvent exchange procedures, there might be an effect of the dispersed nanophase, i.e. the CDs, which is more relevant - when comparable to the pore size of the host matrix.

FT-IR spectroscopy was also used to investigate the features of the composites and to address differences in surface coverage arising from the different production techniques. Mid-IR spectra of the CDN-SiO2 composites in the form of xero-, cryo- and aerogel and the corresponding plain SiO2 are reported in Fig. 4 .

The spectra of each CDN-SiO2 composite is very similar to the corresponding silica matrix, being dominated by the typical vibrations of sol-gel silica, such as the symmetric, asymmetric Si-O-Si stretching and the longitudinal optical mode LO3 at around 795, 1100 and 1200 cm−1 [39]. In addition, peaks associated to hydrophilic silica are clearly present, with particular reference to the xerogels and cryogels, where the vibrations of adsorbed water (at 1640 and around 3500 cm−1) as well as the silanol stretching around 960 cm−1 are observed. On the other hand, the distinctive functional groups observed in pure CDN (Fig. 1 a-c) cannot be unambiguously detected due to overlap of the vibrations of the silica matrix. In the aerogel samples the spectra suggest that a less hydrophilic matrix is present, as expected based on the high temperature supercritical drying procedure used for sample preparation. As pointed by the detected C-H peaks around 2900 cm−1, silanol esterification by ethoxy groups takes place under the high temperature and pressure conditions required for supercritical drying, leading to hydrophobic silica [40]. Thermal analysis also supports the different surface features associated to the drying procedure adopted for gel preparation (see Fig. SI-5). In particular, the xerogel and cryogels are hydrophilic in nature, as shown by the weight loss at low temperature, whereas the aerogels are hydrophobic and show a very limited weight loss at low temperature, and a weight loss ascribed to the combustion of organic groups.

Overall, the textural characterization of the CDN-SiO2 samples demonstrates that the adopted drying method affects the porous texture of the composites. The samples also differ by visual appearance (see Fig. SI-4) which could be related to the different porosity as well as to the fact that in the production of C-CDN-SiO2 (during the solvent exchange procedures required for subsequent lyophilization) as well as A-CDN-SiO2 (during the supercritical drying) some CD release from the matrix was detected.

The functional properties of the developed CDN-SiO2 composites were assessed by photoluminescence (PL) measurements. To take into account PL contribution from the silica matrix, the plain SiO2 aero-, cryo-, and xerogels were also investigated. Fig. 5 clearly indicates that both the aerogel and the cryogel composites have a significant emission peaked at 460 nm, whereas the X-CDN-SiO2 exhibits an emission peaked at higher wavelengths, around 520 nm. Comparison with the corresponding plain SiO2 matrices indicates (see Fig. SI-6) that in the case of A-CDN-SiO2 and C-CDN-SiO2 the PL signal is greatly enhanced in the composites as compared to the corresponding SiO2 matrix. In particular, taking into account that plain porous silica has a fluorescence emission in the blue range [41], insertion of fluorescent CDN leads to a ~100 fold PL increase in the aerogel and ~10 fold increase in the cryogel. On the other hand, the X-CDN-SiO2 shows a PL signal of similar intensity to the corresponding X-SiO2 matrix. Although these emission efficiencies are just qualitative, these results can be interpreted taking into account that the X-CDN-SiO2 sample features a microporous texture and relatively higher CDN loading. Therefore, as reported for solid carbon dots and supported carbon dots in composites with high loadings, aggregation-induced quenching takes place [22,27]. On the other hand, in the aerogel and in the cryogel the carbon dots are more effectively dispersed within the matrix and the PL properties of the carbon dots are retained at the solid state. In particular, the A-CDN-SiO2 due to the very large pores enables to avoid aggregation of CDN and hence stronger fluorescence is achieved. This interpretation is indeed confirmed by the analysis of the spectral emission, which undergoes a red shift going from aero- to cryo- and finally xero-samples. Indeed, the emission band in aero-CDN composites (larger pore dimensions) is spectrally like the one of free CDN in water (Fig. 2c), whilst it red-shifts to green emission as the pore dimensions decrease (to cryo and finally xero-composites), in agreement with previous investigations on CDN-SiO2 materials [14]. The analysis of the emission spectra on the energy space by two Gaussian bands deconvolution shows the presence of two bands (a blue and a green one) whose relative contribution changes as the sample porosity changes, leading to the red shift observed.

Two emitting species are frequently reported in carbon dots, with particular reference to those prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid and nitrogen-based molecules, as in the present case. Typically, a blue and a green contribution are singled out, sometimes an additional yellow/red one is also reported [6,37,42,43]. In the present case, the fitting of the experimental data points at two emission bands, a blue one and a green one, with different relative contribution in the xero-, cryo- and aerogel composites. The excitation spectra at the emission peaks reported in Fig. 6 point out that despite the small peak shift (490 nm in C-CDN-SiO2 and 515 in X-CDN-SiO2) the green band extracted in xerogel and cryogel CDN -SiO2 samples is the same band, being the excitation spectrum the same in the two composites. The excitation spectra also reveal that the blue and green bands are related to two different emitting centers, since the green band displays an excitation channel in the 400–500 nm range that is not observed for the blue band.

In addition, the two bands share a common excitation channel peaked at about 350 nm, in good agreement with the corresponding absorption peak. This excitation channel shows a small red shift of the 350 nm peak at increasing emission wavelengths, recalling a typical feature of CDN samples (vide infra). Most accredited models for the observed CDs emissions include the molecular model, the core state emission and the surface state emission [2–4]. In general, blue and green emission are related to both molecular and surface state models, the core states being assigned to UV/blue contributions sometimes reported when exciting in the UV range. Indeed, the presence of specific polycyclic aromatic molecules, such as IPCA, HPPT (imidazo[1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid, 1,2,3,5-tetrahydro-5-oxo- and 4-hydroxy-1H-pyrrolo[3,4-c]pyridine-1,3,6(2H,5H)-trione, respectively) and citrazinic acid, were recently reported as responsible for the emission features of carbon dots and the excitation-dependence of their emission [34,37,42,44]. The emitting molecules are typically formed during the thermal degradation of the CD precursors in bottom-up synthesis. These preparation methods can also lead to the formation of molecular aggregates or by-products which are also fluorescent in the blue-green range, providing an additional contribution to the CDs emission [43,45]. The polycyclic aromatic molecules and the related aggregates are expected to be both at the core and at the surface of the carbon nanoparticles. Recently, we were able to interpret the emission of carbon dots by associating the blue emission to intrinsic molecule-like centers and the green emission to an extrinsic surface state center [14]. We also reported the relative increase of the green emission as compared to the blue one upon aggregation of carbon dots. In the present case, the matrix porosity controls the average distance between neighboring CDN, leading to different CDs aggregation depending on the pore size and volume. An explanation of the reduced efficiency and red-shifted emission of the CDN could be the formation of molecular aggregates of citrazinic acid derivatives. These molecules can form both non-emitting molecular aggregates, such as H-dimers, and emitting ones, such as J-dimers, which however show a red-shifted emission with respect to the monomers. The formation of non-emitting centers and consequently of larger amounts of non-radiative decay paths is also confirmed by the analysis of the experimental decay times.

The second decay is associated to the emission spectrum as a whole, since we were not able to discriminate between the decay of the blue emission and the decay of the green one. It can be noted that this decay time decreases as the pore size decreases, supporting the idea of relative increase of non-radiative pathways due to the formation of aggregates. In addition, these non-radiative pathways also account for the possible phonon mediated interaction between the CDN and the silica matrix. The estimated decay times are average values, being extracted from the overall PL signal integrated over the spectral band, therefore it is useful to define a weighted mean lifetime as reported in Table 2 with the fractional contribution of each component. The mean lifetime is largely reduced in the xerogel composite, where the fractional contribution of the fast decay exceeds 90%, in agreement with the hypothesis of a quenching mechanism. It is interesting to note that, despite the efficiency reduction in the cryogel composite as compared to the aerogel, the fractional contribution of the two components are very similar in the two samples. Since subnanosecond decays were related to the interaction of the emitting centers with optical and acoustic phonons while nanosecond decays were assigned to electron−hole recombinations at the same centers [48], this is a clear indication that a completely different kinetics rules the photophysics of the different composites. Indeed, in the interplay between phonon scattering and recombination mechanism, the former is the prevalent one in X-CDN-SiO2 samples, whereas the latter is indeed competitive in the A-CDN-SiO2 and C-CDN-SiO2 ones.

Overall, the obtained values suggest that the aero- and cryogel undergo a similar emission mechanism, although slightly larger decay times were recorded for the A-CDN-SiO2 sample. On the other hand, the PL decay of X-CDN-SiO2 is dominated by quite faster decay times, corroborating previous data on the different emissive behavior of the xerogel sample. These data are consistent with the fact that the emission properties of CDN are modulated when inserted in the macroporous and microporous silica matrices, leading to red shift of the emission and decrease of the overall intensity due to aggregation induced effects when the pores dimension decreases.

The data above suggest therefore that an effective way to retain and stabilize the emissive behavior of CDN and produce solid state devices relies in their dispersion in a highly porous matrix where the pore size enables to prevent quenching of the fluorescence, such as aerogels and, partly, cryogels. Large pore volumes associated to meso- and macropores have also found to be desirable in the design of silica and other ceramics for applications where effective mass transport through pores and channels is required [49]. Although some challenges are found in the production of composites by supercritical or freeze drying as opposed to conventional thermal treatments, as it was found that some CDN loss may take place during solvent exchange or removal, it was found that no leakage of the CD took place from the final composite. In particular, the CDN-SiO2 composite powders were stirred with excess water and left in contact overnight. After that contact time, we observed that the supernatant water was colorless under daylight and slightly colored under UV light (see Fig. SI-8). These data suggest a durable and reliable behavior of the prepared CDN-SiO2 composites through the adopted co-gelation of CD and silica matrix, as CD leakage is undesired for application involving a liquid analyte such as environmental and biomedical sensing [50].

The production of emissive composites based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDN) on micro-mesoporous silica matrix was investigated. It was found that in the X-CDN-SiO2 composite the fluorescence encountered for the starting carbon dots dispersions is largely decreased and red-shifted to the green range. This is ascribed to the detrimental effect of the fine microporous texture, which leads to a relatively denser composite where the emissive centers are close to each other. On the other hand, when larger porosities are achieved, such as in C-CDN-SiO2 and A-CDN-SiO2, the open texture enables effective dispersion of the CDN, and fluorescent composites were successfully obtained. Due to the unique extended and large mesoporosity, the aerogel composites represent the most efficient emissive composites and can also be obtained in the form of monoliths.

This study demonstrates that the practical use of fluorescent nanoparticles such as carbon dots, which hold great potential due to tunable and strong emission combined to readily available low-cost precursors, can be extended to application in solid state by incorporation in highly porous matrices to attain chemical and optical stability.

Carlo Maria Carbonaro: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Swapneel Vijay Thakkar: Investigation, Data curation. Róbert Ludmerczki: Investigation, Data curation. Chiara Olla: Data curation. Andrea Pinna: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Danilo Loche: Investigation, Data curation. Luca Malfatti: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Flaminia Cesare Marincola: Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Maria Francesca Casula: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Visual appearance, thermogravimetric measurements, and relevant FT-IR spectral regions for the CDN-SiO2 composites. Characterization of SiO2 xero-, cryo-, and aero-gel: visual appearance, N2 physisorption characterization and Transmission electron microscopy images. Photoluminescence spectra for the CDN-SiO2 composites and plain silica samples and decay time plots for SiO2 samples and details of the multiexponential decay model.
Supplementary data Supplementary data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.micromeso.2020.110302.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mura, L. Stagi, L. Malfatti, C.M. Carbonaro, R. Ludmerczki, P. Innocenzi, Modulating the Optical Properties of Citrazinic Acid through the Monomer-to-Dimer Transformation, J. Phys. Chem. A. 124 (2020) 197-203. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.jpca.9b10884.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence characterization of aged and regenerated mesoporous silica

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.M. Carbonaro, P.C. Ricci, R. Corpino, M. Marceddu, A. Anedda, Photoluminescence characterization of aged and regenerated mesoporous silica, in: J. Non. Cryst. Solids, North-Holland, 2011: pp. 1904-1907. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jnoncrysol.2010.11.107.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intrinsic and extrinsic fluorescence in carbon nanodots: ultrafast time-resolved fluorescence and carrier dynamics
Adv. Opt. Mater.

Chem. Mater.

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
